1.hint: Round, no corners — name the shape.
1.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Name the round 2D shape with no corners.
2. A **circle** is round with **no vertices**.
3. Use this check: wheel outline.

2.hint: Middle point of a circle — special name?
2.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: What is the point in the exact middle of a circle called?
2. The **centre** is the middle point equidistant from the edge.
3. Use this check: compass needle point.

3.hint: Every point on the edge — same distance from centre?
3.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? Every point on the edge of a circle is the same distance from its centre.
2. Yes — all points on the **[circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference)** are the same distance from the centre. **True**.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) constant round the circle.

4.hint: Centre to edge — radius or diameter?
4.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A line goes from the centre to the edge of a circle. Is it a [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) or a [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)?
2. Centre to edge is a **[radius](mtlearn:word/radius)**.
3. Use this check: spoke on a wheel.

5.hint: Across through the centre — radius or diameter?
5.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A line goes across a circle through its centre. Is it a [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) or a [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)?
2. Through the centre edge to edge is a **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)**.
3. Use this check: widest part of a circle.

6.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) is 3 cm. A [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) is two radii. Find the [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 2 \times 3 = 6$ cm.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 5 cm → [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 10 cm.

7.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) is 10 cm. Find the [radius](mtlearn:word/radius).
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) $= 10 \div 2 = 5$ cm.
3. Use this check: [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 14 cm → [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 7 cm.

8.hint: Outside edge of a circle — one word.
8.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: What word names the outside edge of a circle?
2. The **[circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference)** is the distance around the outside.
3. Use this check: rim of a cup.

9.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Name the middle point of a circle.
2. The **centre**.
3. Use this check: dot at middle of a circle.

10.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Name the distance from the centre to the edge of a circle.
2. **[Radius](mtlearn:word/radius)** — distance from centre to [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference).
3. Use this check: 4 cm [radius](mtlearn:word/radius).

11.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Name the distance straight across a circle, through its centre.
2. **[Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** — twice the [radius](mtlearn:word/radius).
3. Use this check: 12 cm [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) → 6 cm [radius](mtlearn:word/radius).

12.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Complete: [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) = 2 × __________.
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 2 \times$ **[radius](mtlearn:word/radius)**.
3. Use this check: $d = 2r$.

13.hint: Distance all the way round a circle.
13.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Name the distance all the way around a circle.
2. **[Circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference)** — perimeter of a circle.
3. Use this check: measure rim length.

14.hint: Touches at one point only, does not cut through.
14.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Name a straight line that touches a circle at exactly one point, without crossing it.
2. A **tangent** touches the circle at **one point**.
3. Use this check: ladder just touching a round tower.

15.hint: Straight line joining two points on the edge.
15.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Name a straight line joining two points on a circle's [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference).
2. A **chord** joins two points on the **[circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference)**.
3. Use this check: string stretched between two rim points.

16.hint: Part of the curved edge between two points.
16.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Name a part of a circle's [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference) between two points.
2. An **arc** is part of the **[circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference)** between two points.
3. Use this check: rainbow arc.

17.hint: Two radii and an arc — pizza slice name.
17.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Name the "pizza slice" region enclosed between two radii and an arc.
2. A **sector** — region between two radii and an arc.
3. Use this check: pizza slice from centre.

18.hint: Region between a chord and an arc — not through centre.
18.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Name the region enclosed between a chord and an arc.
2. A **segment** — [area](mtlearn:word/area) between **chord** and **arc**.
3. Use this check: cap cut off a circle.

19.hint: Diameter joins two points on the circle — is it a chord?
19.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? A [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) is also a chord.
2. A [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) is a chord through the **centre** — **true**.
3. Use this check: longest chord.

20.hint: Tangent and radius at contact point — what angle?
20.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: What angle does a tangent always make with the [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) drawn to the point where it touches the circle?
2. **90°** — tangent **perpendicular** to [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) at touch point.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) to tangent forms right angle.

21.hint: Segment from centre to edge — name?
21.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: What is the name of a line segment from the centre to the circle?
2. **[Radius](mtlearn:word/radius)**.
3. Use this check: 7 cm [radius](mtlearn:word/radius).

22.hint: Line segment with both ends on the circle.
22.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: What is a chord?
2. A **chord** connects two points on the [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference).
3. Use this check: not necessarily through centre.

23.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A circle has [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 6 cm. Find its [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 2 \times 6 = 12$ cm.
3. Use this check: r=8 → d=16.

24.hint: Why 90° at tangent touch point — shortest distance to line.
24.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Explain why a tangent to a circle is always perpendicular to the [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) drawn to the point of contact.
2. The [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) to the touch point is the **shortest** line to the tangent, so it meets at **90°**.
3. Use this check: sketch [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) to tangent.

25.hint: Chord splits circle into two regions with arc + chord.
25.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A chord divides a circle into two regions, each made from the chord and an arc. What are these two regions called?
2. Two **segments** (minor and major) — each bounded by chord and arc.
3. Use this check: chord not through centre → unequal segments.

26.hint: Longest chord passes through centre — always?
26.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? A [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) of a circle is the longest possible chord.
2. **[Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** is longest chord — **always** true.
3. Use this check: any other chord shorter.

27.hint: Two tangents from outside point to circle — compare lengths.
27.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Two tangents are drawn to a circle from the same point outside the circle. What is always true about the lengths of these two tangents?
2. Tangent segments from one **external point** are **equal** in length.
3. Use this check: two tangents from P to circle.

28.hint: Radius to centre; chord edge to edge — not the same.
28.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Sam says, "A [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) and a chord are the same thing." Is Sam correct? Explain the difference between them.
2. Sam is **wrong**: **[radius](mtlearn:word/radius)** centre to edge; **chord** between two edge points.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 5 cm vs chord 8 cm.

29.hint: 90° sector — what fraction of full 360° turn?
29.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. What fraction of the whole circle is the shaded sector?
2. $90 \div 360 = \frac{1}{4}$ of the circle.
3. Use this check: quarter pizza.

30.hint: Sector includes centre; segment does not.
30.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Explain the difference between a segment and a sector of a circle.
2. **Sector**: two **radii** + arc (pie slice). **Segment**: **chord** + arc (no centre corner).
3. Use this check: label both on a diagram.

31.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A circular table has [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 1.4 m. Find its [radius](mtlearn:word/radius).
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) $= 1.4 \div 2 = 0.7$ m.
3. Use this check: table [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 2 m → r=1 m.

32.hint: Path once around the track — distance name.
32.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A cyclist rides once around a perfectly circular track. Name the circle feature that matches the path traced by their front tyre.
2. One lap is the **[circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference)**.
3. Use this check: tyre tracks full loop.

33.hint: Two tangents from outside — equal tangent lengths.
33.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A satellite dish has two support struts, both tangent to the dish's circular rim, meeting at a point outside the dish. One strut is 1.6 m long. Using the equ…
2. Tangent segments from the external point to the circle are **equal**.
3. Use this check: symmetric struts to rim.

34.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A pizza is cut into 6 equal sector slices from the centre. Find the angle at the centre of each slice.
2. Centre angle each slice $= 360 \div 6 = 60°$.
3. Use this check: 8 slices → 45° each.

35.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A clock's minute hand is a [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) of length 8 cm. Find the length of the [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) drawn through the centre and the tip of the hand's opposite point.
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 2 \times 8 = 16$ cm.
3. Use this check: through centre and minute hand tip.

36.hint: Straight line across semicircle flat end.
36.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A running track has a semicircular end. Name the circle feature that describes the straight line running across the flat side of that semicircle, through the…
2. The flat end is a **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** (or **chord** of full circle).
3. Use this check: semicircle base line.

37.hint: Shortest from centre to tangent — along radius at 90°.
37.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Explain, using the tangent–[radius](mtlearn:word/radius) right angle, why the shortest distance from the centre of a circle to a tangent line is always exactly equal to the [radius](mtlearn:word/radius).
2. Shortest distance is the **[radius](mtlearn:word/radius)** to the touch point, perpendicular to tangent.
3. Use this check: compare slant line vs [radius](mtlearn:word/radius).

38.hint: External touch — centres apart by sum of radii.
38.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Two circles have radii 5 cm and 3 cm and touch each other externally at a single point (they don't overlap). Find the distance between their two centres.
2. Distance between centres $= 5 + 3 = 8$ cm.
3. Use this check: two circles touching externally.

39.hint: Chord 6 cm from centre, radius 10 — half-chord 3, Pythagoras.
39.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A circle has [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 10 cm. A chord is drawn 6 cm from the centre. Using the right-angled triangle formed by the [radius](mtlearn:word/radius), half the chord, and the 6 cm distance…
2. Half chord $= 3$ cm. Chord length $= 2 \times \sqrt{10^2 - 6^2} = 2 \times 8 = 16$ cm.
3. Use this check: right triangle [radius](mtlearn:word/radius), distance, half-chord.

40.hint: Recall the tangent–radius theorem at the touching point.
40.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) is drawn to the point where a tangent touches a circle. Describe the angle between the [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) and the tangent.
2. A tangent touches a circle at exactly one point. The [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) drawn to that point is perpendicular to the tangent.
3. Perpendicular lines meet at a right angle, which is 90°.

41.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) is 5 cm. What is the [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)?
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) **10 cm**.
3. Use this check: r=5 → d=10.

42.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Name the line that touches a circle at exactly one point.
2. **Tangent**.
3. Use this check: line just grazing circle.

43.hint: Which circle word matches: centre to the circle?
43.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Name the circle part: centre to the circle.
2. A **[radius](mtlearn:word/radius)** is the part that centre to the circle.
3. Sketch a circle and label it.

44.hint: Which circle word matches: through the centre, end to end?
44.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Name the circle part: through the centre, end to end.
2. A **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** is the part that through the centre, end to end.
3. Sketch a circle and label it.

45.hint: Diameter is twice the radius — double 9 cm.
45.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A circle has [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 9 cm. Find the [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 2 \times 9 = 18$ cm.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 6 cm → [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 12 cm.

46.hint: Radius is half the diameter — divide 14 cm by 2.
46.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) is 14 cm. Find the [radius](mtlearn:word/radius).
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) $= 14 \div 2 = 7$ cm.
3. Use this check: [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 20 cm → [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 10 cm.

47.hint: A diameter joins two points on the circle through the centre — is it also a chord?
47.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? Every [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) is a chord.
2. A **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** is a **chord** that passes through the **centre** — **true**.
3. Use this check: any chord is a line segment between two points on the circle.

48.hint: Which line touches the circle at exactly one point without crossing inside?
48.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Name the line that touches a circle at exactly one point.
2. A **tangent** touches the circle at **one point** only.
3. Use this check: a wheel on flat ground — ground line is tangent at the contact.

49.hint: All radii of one circle go from centre to edge — are they the same length?
49.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Two radii of a circle are always equal in length. True or false?
2. Every **[radius](mtlearn:word/radius)** of the same circle has the **same length** — **true**.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 5 cm from centre to any point on the circle.

50.hint: Sector fraction: divide central angle 72° by 360°.
50.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A sector has central angle 72°. What fraction of the full circle is it?
2. Fraction $= 72 \div 360 = 1/5$ of the full circle.
3. Use this check: 90° sector → $90/360 = 1/4$.

51.hint: Eight equal pizza slices from the centre — divide 360° by 8.
51.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A pizza is cut into 8 equal slices from the centre. Central angle per slice?
2. Central angle per slice $= 360 \div 8 = 45°$.
3. Use this check: 6 equal slices → 60° each.

52.hint: Radius meets tangent at the contact point — what angle do they make?
52.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Tangent meets [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) at the contact point. What angle do they make?
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) and tangent are **perpendicular** → **90°** at the contact point.
3. Use this check: a square corner fits between [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) and tangent.

53.hint: The diameter is the longest chord — can any chord be longer than a diameter?
53.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? A chord can be longer than a [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).
2. No chord can be **longer than a [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** — **never**.
3. Use this check: [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 10 cm is the maximum chord length.

54.hint: Circumference uses $\pi d$ — find diameter from radius 11 cm first.
54.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Circle [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 11 cm. [Circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference) formula uses $\pi d$ — find the [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) first.
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 2 \times 11 = 22$ cm, then use $C = \pi d$.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 5 cm → $d = 10$ cm.

55.hint: From an external point, two tangents are drawn to a circle — how do their lengths compare?
55.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: From a point outside a circle, two tangents are drawn. What is true about their lengths?
2. The two tangents from the same external point are **equal in length**.
3. Use this check: both tangents from P might be 8 cm each.

56.hint: Semicircle flat edge across the middle — what is that straight line called?
56.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A semicircle is half a circle. What is the [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) called on the flat edge?
2. The flat edge of a semicircle is the **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)**.
3. Use this check: half a circle cut through the centre.

57.hint: Does the centre point lie on every chord of a circle?
57.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? The centre lies on every chord.
2. Only a **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** passes through the centre — ordinary chords need **not** contain the centre — **false**.
3. Use this check: a short chord misses the centre.

58.hint: Compare chord 8 cm with diameter 10 cm (radius 5 cm) — which is longer?
58.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 5 cm. A chord is 8 cm long. Is the chord shorter than the [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)?
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 10$ cm. Chord **8 cm < 10 cm**, so the chord is **shorter**.
3. Use this check: longest chord equals [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).

59.hint: Sector with central angle 120° — what fraction of 360°?
59.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. What fraction of the circle is the sector?
2. Fraction $= 120 \div 360 = 1/3$ of the circle.
3. Use this check: 180° sector is half the circle.

60.hint: Two circles with the same radius — identical size circles?
60.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? Two circles with the same [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) are congruent.
2. Equal radii → same size circles → **congruent** — **always**.
3. Use this check: both [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 4 cm circles match exactly.

61.hint: Quarter turn of minute hand — what fraction of the full circumference does the tip travel?
61.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: A clock minute hand is 10 cm ([radius](mtlearn:word/radius)). Through what distance does the tip travel for a quarter turn? (arc length fraction of [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference))
2. Quarter turn $= 1/4$ of the [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference). Arc length $= \frac{1}{4} \times 2\pi r$ with $r = 10$ cm.
3. Use this check: half turn → half the [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference).

62.hint: Two tangents from the same point P — if one is 12 cm, what is the other?
62.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the table. A tangent from point P has one length shown. What is the length of the second tangent from P?
2. Equal tangents property: second tangent is also **12 cm**.
3. Use this check: both tangents from P are 7 cm.

63.hint: Is an arc a curved part of the circumference?
63.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? An arc is part of the [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference).
2. An **arc** is part of the **[circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference)** — **true**.
3. Use this check: the curved edge from A to B on a circle.

64.hint: Radius is half the diameter — find radius when diameter is 20 cm.
64.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Circle [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 20 cm. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius)?
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) $= 20 \div 2 = 10$ cm.
3. Use this check: [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 14 cm → [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 7 cm.

65.hint: Segment vs sector — which region includes the centre of the circle?
65.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Segment vs sector: which includes the centre?
2. A **sector** has two radii and includes the **centre**. A **segment** is between a chord and arc — usually **no centre**.
3. Use this check: pizza slice is a sector.

66.hint: If a radius is perpendicular to a chord, does it cut the chord in half?
66.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? A [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) perpendicular to a chord bisects the chord.
2. A [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) **perpendicular** to a chord **bisects** the chord — **always** in the same circle.
3. Use this check: symmetry through the centre.

67.hint: Circles touch externally — add radii 4 cm and 7 cm for centre distance.
67.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Two circles radii 4 cm and 7 cm touch externally. Distance between centres?
2. Distance between centres $= 4 + 7 = 11$ cm.
3. Use this check: radii 3 and 5 touching externally → 8 cm apart.

68.hint: 90° sector — what fraction of the full circle area?
68.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. The [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) and sector at the centre are shown. What fraction of the circle [area](mtlearn:word/area) is the sector?
2. Fraction $= 90 \div 360 = 1/4$ of the circle [area](mtlearn:word/area).
3. Use this check: 180° sector is half the [area](mtlearn:word/area).

69.hint: Are all points on a circle the same distance from the centre?
69.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? All points on a circle are equidistant from the centre.
2. Every point on a circle is **equidistant** from the **centre** — **true**.
3. Use this check: that distance is the [radius](mtlearn:word/radius).

70.hint: OA joins centre O to point A on the circle — what is OA called?
70.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Chord AB and chord AC share point A on the circle. Is AC a [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) if A is centre? (No centre at A) — instead: if OA is [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) to A, is OA a [radius](mtlearn:word/radius)?
2. **OA** is a **[radius](mtlearn:word/radius)** (centre to circle).
3. Use this check: any line from centre to the edge is a [radius](mtlearn:word/radius).

71.hint: Radius 13 cm — double it for the diameter.
71.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Rewrite simpler: A [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) is 13 cm. Find [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 2 \times 13 = 26$ cm.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 8 cm → [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 16 cm.

72.hint: Can a tangent line cross the circle at two points?
72.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? A tangent can cut the circle twice.
2. A **tangent** touches at **one point** only — it cannot cut twice — **never**.
3. Use this check: tangent just grazes the circle.

73.hint: Longest chord in a circle — what special name does it have?
73.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Circle [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 8 cm. Longest chord length?
2. The **longest chord** is the **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** $= 2 \times 8 = 16$ cm.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 5 cm → longest chord 10 cm.

74.hint: 60° sector — divide by 360° for fraction of circumference.
74.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. What fraction of the [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference) is the arc of the sector shown?
2. Arc fraction $= 60 \div 360 = 1/6$ of the [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference).
3. Use this check: 90° → $1/4$ of [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference).

75.hint: Does circumference mean the distance around the edge of a circle?
75.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? The word [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference) means the perimeter of a circle.
2. **[Circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference)** is the perimeter around a circle — **true**.
3. Use this check: measure the edge of a plate.

76.hint: Equal tangents from P — if one tangent is 9 cm, find the other.
76.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Two tangents from P to a circle are equal. One is 9 cm. Other?
2. Both tangents from P are **9 cm**.
3. Use this check: tangents 6 cm and 6 cm from same point.

77.hint: Diameter from radius 12 cm — multiply by 2.
77.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 12 cm. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)?
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 2 \times 12 = 24$ cm.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 9 cm → [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 18 cm.

78.hint: Must every diameter pass through the centre of the circle?
78.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? A [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) passes through the centre.
2. A **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** goes through the **centre** end to end — **always**.
3. Use this check: centre is the midpoint of a [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).

79.hint: Chord 10 cm in a circle whose diameter is 10 cm — is it a diameter?
79.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Chord length 10 cm in circle [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 10 cm. Is the chord a [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)?
2. A chord equal to the **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** **is** a [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) — **yes**.
3. Use this check: longest possible chord equals [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).

80.hint: Sector angle 45° — fraction of full 360° turn?
80.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. What fraction of the circle is the sector?
2. Fraction $= 45 \div 360 = 1/8$.
3. Use this check: 120° → $1/3$ of circle.

81.hint: Does a sector look like a slice of pizza from the centre?
81.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? A sector looks like a pizza slice.
2. A **sector** is a **pizza-slice** shape with two radii and an arc — **true**.
3. Use this check: wedge cut from centre of round pizza.

82.hint: Circles touch internally — subtract smaller radius 3 cm from larger 5 cm.
82.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Circles touch internally: radii 5 cm and 3 cm same centre line. Distance between centres?
2. Centre distance $= 5 - 3 = 2$ cm.
3. Use this check: radii 8 and 5 internally → 3 cm apart.

83.hint: Find diameter when radius is 7 cm.
83.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 7 cm. Find [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 2 \times 7 = 14$ cm.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 4.5 cm → [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 9 cm.

84.hint: Tangents drawn from the same point outside the circle — equal lengths?
84.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? Equal tangents from an external point.
2. Tangents from one **external point** are **equal** — **always**.
3. Use this check: both 11 cm from P.

85.hint: Minor arc AB has a longer partner from A to B — what is it called?
85.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Arc AB is minor arc. What is the other arc from A to B called?
2. The longer path is the **major arc**.
3. Use this check: minor arc is the shorter curve between A and B.

86.hint: Radius to the tangent contact point — perpendicular to the tangent?
86.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? The [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) to a tangent point is perpendicular to the tangent.
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) ⟂ tangent at the point of contact — **true** (90°).
3. Use this check: square corner at contact point.

87.hint: Diameter 30 cm — is chord 24 cm shorter than the diameter?
87.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Circle [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 15 cm. Chord 24 cm — is 24 < [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 30?
2. 24 cm **<** 30 cm [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) — chord is **shorter** — **yes**.
3. Use this check: no chord exceeds [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) length.

88.hint: Central angle 180° sector — half a circle — what shape name?
88.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. The sector shown is what fraction of a full circle? What shape name fits?
2. 180° sector is a **semicircle**.
3. Use this check: half a round pizza.

89.hint: Can two different chords in the same circle have the same length?
89.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? Two chords can be equal in length.
2. **Equal chords** are possible in one circle — **always** (symmetry).
3. Use this check: two 6 cm chords opposite each other.

90.hint: Line from centre O to point A on the circle — name OA.
90.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: From centre O, OA = 9 cm. Point A is on the circle. Name OA.
2. A segment from the **centre** to any point on the circle is a **[radius](mtlearn:word/radius)**. So **OA** is a [radius](mtlearn:word/radius).
3. Use this check: OB or OC from the centre are also radii.

91.hint: Is a segment the region trapped between a chord and an arc?
91.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? A segment is region between chord and arc.
2. A **segment** is the region between a **chord** and an **arc** — **true**.
3. Use this check: a flat cap cut off a circle.

92.hint: Diameter 1.8 m — halve it and convert metres to centimetres.
92.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 1.8 m. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) in cm?
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) $= 0.9$ m $= 90$ cm.
3. Use this check: [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 2 m → [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 100 cm.

93.hint: External touch — add radii 6 cm and 2 cm for distance between centres.
93.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Two circles radii 6 and 2 touch externally. Centre distance?
2. Distance $= 6 + 2 = 8$ cm.
3. Use this check: radii 5 and 4 external → 9 cm.

94.hint: Line through centre meeting circle at both ends — is it a diameter?
94.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? A line through the centre is a [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) if it meets the circle at both ends.
2. A line through the **centre** hitting the circle twice is a **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** — **always**.
3. Use this check: widest chord through centre.

95.hint: Angle between radius and tangent at the point of contact?
95.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Tangent–[radius](mtlearn:word/radius) angle at contact?
2. Tangent ⟂ [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) → **90°**.
3. Use this check: square corner at contact point.

96.hint: Is $\pi$ used in the formula for circle circumference?
96.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? $\pi$ is used for circle [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference).
2. $C = \pi d$ or $C = 2\pi r$ — **true** that **π** is used.
3. Use this check: [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference) of a wheel uses π.

97.hint: 30° sector — what fraction of a full 360° turn?
97.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. What fraction of a full turn is the sector?
2. Fraction $= 30 \div 360 = 1/12$.
3. Use this check: 60° → $1/6$ of circle.

98.hint: Longest possible chord when radius is 20 cm?
98.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 20 cm. Longest chord?
2. Longest chord $=$ **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** $= 40$ cm.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 7 cm → longest chord 14 cm.

99.hint: Concentric circles — do they share the same centre?
99.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? Concentric circles share a centre.
2. **Concentric** circles have the **same centre** — **always**.
3. Use this check: target rings on a dartboard.

100.hint: Chord 6 cm, radius 5 cm — half chord 3, radius 5: can a right triangle work?
100.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Chord 6 cm, [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 5 cm — can this happen? (Think 3-4-5 half chord)
2. Half chord 3, [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 5: $3^2 + 4^2 = 5^2$ — distance from centre to chord is 4 cm — **yes, possible**.
3. Use this check: 3-4-5 triangle inside circle.

101.hint: Where does the centre of a circle sit — inside or outside?
101.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? The centre is inside the circle.
2. The **centre** is **inside** the circle — **true**.
3. Use this check: compass needle point sits inside the drawn circle.

102.hint: Diameter 22 cm — halve it to find the radius.
102.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 22 cm. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius)?
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) $= 22 \div 2 = 11$ cm.
3. Use this check: [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 16 cm → [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 8 cm.

103.hint: Two tangents both 15 cm from P — are tangents from one point equal?
103.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Two tangents 15 cm each from P. True or false? tangents equal.
2. Tangents from the same **external point** are **equal** — **true**.
3. Use this check: both 9 cm from P.

104.hint: Half a circle — what fraction of the full circumference?
104.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Arc length for half a circle is what fraction of [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference)?
2. Half a circle is **$1/2$** of the [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference).
3. Use this check: semicircle arc is half the edge.

105.hint: Does a sector always have two radii from the centre?
105.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? A sector contains two radii.
2. A **sector** is bounded by **two radii** and an arc — **always**.
3. Use this check: pizza slice has two straight edges from centre.

106.hint: Two radii 4 cm apart at 90° from the centre — name the bounded shape.
106.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. Two radii from the same centre are shown with the angle between them. Name the shape bounded by the radii and the arc.
2. Two radii at **90°** make a **quarter circle** (quadrant).
3. Use this check: one slice of a four-slice pizza.

107.hint: Does a chord join two points on the circle's edge?
107.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? A chord joins two points on the circle.
2. A **chord** joins **two points on the circle** — **true**.
3. Use this check: straight line across inside a circle.

108.hint: External touch — add radii 10 cm and 3 cm for centre distance.
108.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Radii 10 cm and 3 cm external touch. Centre distance?
2. Distance $= 10 + 3 = 13$ cm.
3. Use this check: radii 7 and 4 external → 11 cm.

109.hint: 144° sector — divide by 360° for the fraction of the circle.
109.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. What fraction of the circle is the sector?
2. Fraction $= 144 \div 360 = 2/5$.
3. Use this check: 72° → $1/5$ of circle.

110.hint: Is diameter always twice the radius?
110.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) is twice [radius](mtlearn:word/radius).
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 2 \times$ [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) — **always**.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 5 cm → [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 10 cm.

111.hint: Radius OT to tangent at T — what angle between them?
111.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Tangent at T, [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) OT. Angle OT and tangent?
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) ⟂ tangent at contact → **90°**.
3. Use this check: square corner at T.

112.hint: Which arc is shorter — minor or major?
112.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? Minor arc is the shorter path along the [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference).
2. The **minor arc** is the **shorter** path along the [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference) — **true**.
3. Use this check: minor arc is the small curve between A and B.

113.hint: Diameter 50 cm — halve to get radius.
113.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 50 cm. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius)?
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) $= 50 \div 2 = 25$ cm.
3. Use this check: [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 18 cm → [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 9 cm.

114.hint: Equal tangents from P — if one is 7.2 cm, find the other.
114.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Equal tangents property: both tangents from P are 7.2 cm. Second length?
2. Both tangents from P are **7.2 cm**.
3. Use this check: tangents 5 cm and 5 cm from same point.

115.hint: Can radii of the same circle have different lengths?
115.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? All radii of one circle are equal.
2. All **radii** of one circle are **equal** — **always**.
3. Use this check: every spoke same length on a wheel.

116.hint: How many 40° sectors fit into a full 360° turn?
116.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. How many sectors of the size shown would fill a full 360° circle?
2. $360 \div 40 = 9$ sectors fit.
3. Use this check: eight 45° slices make a full pizza.

117.hint: Is a semicircle both a segment and a sector?
117.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? A semicircle is a segment and a sector.
2. A **semicircle** is both a **segment** and a **sector** — **true**.
3. Use this check: half pizza is slice and cap shape.

118.hint: Radius 6 cm — double it for diameter.
118.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 6 cm. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)?
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 2 \times 6 = 12$ cm.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 10 cm → [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 20 cm.

119.hint: Same centre, radii 5 and 8 — are the circles concentric?
119.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Two circles same centre, radii 5 and 8. Are they concentric?
2. Same **centre**, different radii → **concentric** — **yes**.
3. Use this check: dartboard rings share one centre.

120.hint: Does a tangent stay outside the circle except at one touch point?
120.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? A tangent lies entirely outside except at one point.
2. A **tangent** touches at **one point** only — rest is outside — **always**.
3. Use this check: line just grazing a ball.

121.hint: Chord 12 cm, diameter 20 cm — is the chord shorter than the diameter?
121.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Chord 12 cm in circle [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 10 cm — [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) is 20, chord shorter?
2. 12 cm **<** 20 cm [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) — chord is **shorter** — **yes**.
3. Use this check: no chord beats [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) length.

122.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? [Circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference) = $\pi \times$ [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).
2. $C = \pi d$ — **true**.
3. Use this check: wheel rim length uses $\pi \times$ width.

123.hint: 270° sector — what fraction of the full 360° circle?
123.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. What fraction of the circle is the sector?
2. Fraction $= 270 \div 360 = 3/4$.
3. Use this check: 90° → $1/4$ of circle.

124.hint: Both tangents from P are 4 cm — what property links them?
124.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Tangents from P length 4 cm and 4 cm. Property name?
2. **Equal tangents** from an external point — both **4 cm**.
3. Use this check: tangents from P always match in length.

125.hint: Is the longest chord in any circle always a diameter?
125.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? The longest chord is a [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).
2. The **longest chord** is the **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** — **always**.
3. Use this check: widest line through centre.

126.hint: Radius 25 cm — multiply by 2 for diameter.
126.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 25 cm. Find [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 2 \times 25 = 50$ cm.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 12 cm → [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 24 cm.

127.hint: Does an arc start and end at points on the circle?
127.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? An arc has two endpoints on the circle.
2. An **arc** has **two endpoints** on the circle — **true**.
3. Use this check: curved edge between A and B.

128.hint: External touch — add radii 9 cm and 4 cm.
128.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Touching circles radii 9 and 4 external. Distance centres?
2. Distance $= 9 + 4 = 13$ cm.
3. Use this check: radii 6 and 5 external → 11 cm.

129.hint: One fifth of a circle — what central angle in degrees?
129.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Angle at centre for one fifth of a circle?
2. $360 \div 5 = 72°$ at the centre.
3. Use this check: eight equal slices → 45° each.

130.hint: Is the radius always perpendicular to the tangent at the contact point?
130.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? Perpendicular [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) to tangent.
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) ⟂ tangent at contact — **always** (90°).
3. Use this check: square corner where [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) meets tangent.

131.hint: Why are both tangents from an outside point the same length?
131.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Explain equal tangents from a point outside the circle.
2. From external point **P**, tangents to a circle are **equal** — symmetric right triangles OTP and OTP′.
3. Use this check: both tangents 6 cm from P.

132.hint: Does a sector include the centre of the circle?
132.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? A sector includes the centre.
2. A **sector** is a wedge from the **centre** — **true**.
3. Use this check: pizza slice point is at the centre.

133.hint: Diameter 3.2 m — halve and convert to centimetres.
133.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 3.2 m. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) cm?
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) $= 1.6$ m $= 160$ cm.
3. Use this check: [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 2.4 m → [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 120 cm.

134.hint: Chord that does not pass through the centre — still called a chord?
134.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Chord not through centre — name it.
2. A chord **not through the centre** is still a **chord** (not a [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)).
3. Use this check: short line across the circle off-centre.

135.hint: Can two circles touch at exactly one point?
135.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? Two circles can touch at exactly one point.
2. Circles can **touch externally or internally** at **one point** — **always** possible.
3. Use this check: two coins just touching edge to edge.

136.hint: 18° sector — divide by 360° for the fraction.
136.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. What fraction of the circle is the sector?

137.hint: Is every point on the circle the same distance from the centre?
137.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? The centre is equidistant from every point on the circle.
2. The **centre** is **equidistant** from all points on the circle — **true**.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) constant round the edge.

138.hint: Radius 14 cm — longest chord equals diameter.

**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 14 cm. Longest chord length?
2. Longest chord $= 2 \times 14 = 28$ cm.

139.hint: Diameter perpendicular to a chord — does it bisect the chord?
139.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? Tangent parallel to a chord through centre — impossible? (Skip) — Instead: a [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) perpendicular to a chord bisects it — true…
2. A **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) perpendicular to a chord** **bisects** it — **true**.
3. Use this check: fold line through centre cuts chord in half.

140.hint: Equal tangents from P with radius ⟂ tangent — what quadrilateral is OTP?
140.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Two tangents from P are equal. If one makes 90° with [radius](mtlearn:word/radius), describe the quadrilateral OTP?
2. Two right angles at T and T′ with equal tangents → **kite** (or symmetric quadrilateral OTP…).
3. Use this check: mirror symmetry about OP.

141.hint: Does $\pi d$ give the circumference of a circle?
141.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? $\pi d$ gives [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference).
2. $C = \pi d$ — **true**.
3. Use this check: [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 10 cm → $C = 10\pi$ cm.

142.hint: Two radii 5 cm with 120° between them — name the sector OAB.
142.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. The radii and central angle of sector OAB are shown. Name the shape OAB (sector or segment).
2. Central angle **120°** makes a **sector** (one-third of a circle).
3. Use this check: three equal pizza slices fill 360°.

143.hint: Can a chord also be a diameter?
143.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? A chord can be a [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).
2. A chord **through the centre** **is** a [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) — **always** possible.
3. Use this check: widest chord across the circle.

144.hint: Two circles both radius 2 cm — are they congruent?
144.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Circle [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 2 cm. Another circle [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 2 cm. Congruent circles?
2. Same **[radius](mtlearn:word/radius)** → **congruent circles** — **yes**.
3. Use this check: two identical coins.

145.hint: Does a segment usually include the centre?
145.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? Segment does not include the centre (usually).
2. A **segment** (chord + arc region) usually **does not** include the centre — **true**.
3. Use this check: small cap cut off the top.

146.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 18 cm. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius)?
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) $= 18 \div 2 = 9$ cm.
3. Use this check: [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 24 cm → [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 12 cm.

147.hint: External touch — add radii 12 cm and 5 cm.
147.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: External touch radii 12 and 5. Centre distance?
2. Distance $= 12 + 5 = 17$ cm.
3. Use this check: radii 8 and 3 external → 11 cm.

148.hint: Are tangents from P to a circle always symmetric about OP?
148.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? Two tangents from P are symmetric.
2. Tangents from **P** are **symmetric** about line **OP** — **always**.
3. Use this check: mirror image left and right tangents.

149.hint: Arc is one quarter of the circle — what central angle?
149.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Arc for ¼ of circle — central angle?
2. Quarter circle → central angle **90°**.
3. Use this check: four equal slices → 90° each.

150.hint: Sketch and name chord, tangent, and radius on one circle.
150.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: In one sentence each, describe chord, tangent, and [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) on a circle.
2. **[Radius](mtlearn:word/radius)**: centre to edge. **Chord**: line between two edge points. **Tangent**: touches edge once.
3. Use this check: label each on a wheel diagram.

151.hint: Is radius half of the diameter?
151.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) is half the [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) $= $ [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $\div 2$ — **true**.
3. Use this check: [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 14 cm → [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 7 cm.

152.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. What fraction of the circle is the sector?
2. Fraction $= 150 \div 360 = 5/12$.
3. Use this check: 60° → $1/6$ of circle.

153.hint: In one circle, do equal chords sit the same distance from the centre?
153.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? Equal chords equidistant from centre in same circle.
2. **Equal chords** are **equidistant** from the centre in the same circle — **always**.
3. Use this check: two 8 cm chords at same depth.

154.hint: One tangent from P is 8 cm — find the other tangent length.
154.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Tangent length 8 cm from P. Other tangent?
2. Both tangents from P are **8 cm**.
3. Use this check: tangents 12 cm and 12 cm from same point.

155.hint: Is circumference the length around the outside edge?
155.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? [Circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference) is a length around the edge.
2. **[Circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference)** is the distance **around the edge** — **true**.
3. Use this check: tape measure round a tin.

156.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 30 cm. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)?
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 2 \times 30 = 60$ cm.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 15 cm → [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 30 cm.

157.hint: Line through centre hitting the circle twice — is it a diameter?
157.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? A line through centre hitting circle twice is [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).
2. Through **centre**, meets circle twice → **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** — **always**.
3. Use this check: widest line across the circle.

158.hint: Central angle 200° — which sector is minor, 200° or 160°?
158.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Minor sector vs major sector for 200° central angle — which is minor?
2. Minor sector has the **smaller** angle: $360 - 200 = 160°$ is **minor** (200° is major).
3. Use this check: pick the smaller wedge.

159.hint: Concentric circles — do they have different radii?
159.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? Concentric circles have different radii.
2. **Concentric** circles share a centre but have **different radii** — **true**.
3. Use this check: dartboard rings of different widths.

160.hint: Two circles radius 7 touch externally — distance between centres?
160.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Two circles radii 7 touch externally. Distance?
2. Distance $= 7 + 7 = 14$ cm.
3. Use this check: two equal coins touching → sum of radii.

161.hint: Tangent meets radius at a square corner — explain with 90°.
161.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Explain tangent ⟂ [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) using a square corner idea.
2. At the contact point, **[radius](mtlearn:word/radius) ⟂ tangent** — like a **square corner** (90°).

162.hint: Can any chord be longer than the diameter?
162.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) is the longest chord.
2. The **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** is the **longest chord** — **always**.
3. Use this check: no line across the circle beats [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) length.

163.hint: Chord 16 cm equals diameter 16 cm — is it a diameter?
163.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Chord 16 cm, circle [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 16 cm. Is it a [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)?
2. Chord length **equals [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** → it **is** a [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) — **yes**.
3. Use this check: widest chord through centre.

164.hint: Is a 90° sector exactly one quarter of the circle?
164.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? A 90° sector is a quarter circle.

3. Use this check: four equal pizza slices.

165.hint: Radius 4.5 cm — multiply by 2 for diameter.
165.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 4.5 cm. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)?
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 2 \times 4.5 = 9$ cm.
3. Use this check: [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 3.5 cm → [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 7 cm.

166.hint: Point P outside the circle — are tangents from P equal?
166.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? Tangents from P equal if P outside circle.
2. From **external point P**, tangents are **equal** — **always**.
3. Use this check: both tangents 10 cm from P.

167.hint: Central angle 300° — find the minor sector angle.
167.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. The major sector central angle is shown. What is the minor sector angle?

3. Use this check: pick the smaller wedge under 180°.

168.hint: Does arc length depend on both radius and central angle?
168.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? Arc length depends on [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) and angle at centre.
2. Arc length grows with **[radius](mtlearn:word/radius)** and **angle at centre** — **true**.
3. Use this check: bigger circle or bigger angle → longer arc.

169.hint: Internal touch — subtract radii 10 cm and 6 cm.

**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Touching internally radii 10 and 6. Distance between centres?

170.hint: Draw a circle and label chord, tangent, and radius clearly.

171.hint: Do both endpoints of a diameter lie on the circle?

172.hint: Sector is one sixth of the circle — central angle?

**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

3. Use this check: six equal slices → 60° each.

173.hint: Can two circles cross at two separate points?
173.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? Two circles can intersect at two points.
2. Circles can **intersect at two points** — **always** possible if close enough.
3. Use this check: two overlapping rings.

174.hint: Equal tangents property — both lengths are 11 cm.
174.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Equal tangents 11 cm. Find both lengths.

3. Use this check: tangents 7 cm and 7 cm from same point.

175.hint: Does the centre give the circle its name and position?
175.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? The centre names the circle.
2. The **centre** fixes the circle's position — **true** (names the circle in diagrams).
3. Use this check: point O labels circle O.

176.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 40 cm. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius)?
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) $= 40 \div 2 = 20$ cm.
3. Use this check: [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) 26 cm → [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 13 cm.

177.hint: Radius to midpoint of a bisected chord — perpendicular?
177.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) to midpoint of chord is perpendicular if chord bisected.
2. [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) to **midpoint of bisected chord** is **perpendicular** to the chord — **always**.

178.hint: Segment vs sector — which includes the centre?

**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

2. **Sector**: wedge from **centre** (two radii + arc). **Segment**: region between **chord and arc**, usually no centre.
3. Use this check: pizza slice vs flat cap.

179.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? $\pi$ is approximately 3.14.
2. $\pi \approx 3.14$ — **true** (close enough for estimates).

180.hint: External touch — add radii 8 cm and 3 cm.

**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Radii 8 and 3 external touch. Centre distance?

3. Use this check: radii 10 and 6 external → 16 cm.

181.hint: Can a tangent share two points with the circle?
181.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

182.hint: How many 36° sectors fill a full 360° turn?

**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Use the circle diagram. How many sectors of the size shown would fill a full circle?

183.hint: Does a diameter split a circle into two semicircles?
183.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? A [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) splits a circle into two semicircles.
2. A **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** cuts the circle into **two semicircles** — **true**.
3. Use this check: half a round pizza on each side.

184.hint: Half chord 5 cm, radius 6 cm — use Pythagoras for distance from centre.
184.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Chord 10 cm [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 6 cm — half chord 5, [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 6, distance from centre? (Pythagoras preview)
2. Right triangle: half chord 5, [radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 6 → distance from centre $= \sqrt{6^2 - 5^2} = \sqrt{11}$ cm.
3. Use this check: 3-4-5 triangle gives distance 4 cm.

185.hint: Circles with equal radii — are they congruent?
185.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

186.hint: Car mirror tangents both 2.1 m — find the other tangent length.
186.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

2. Equal tangents → other length **2.1 m**.
3. Use this check: both tangents 1.5 m from same point.

187.hint: Does a sector's boundary include a curved arc?
187.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

188.hint: Radius 11 cm means diameter 22 cm — use that for circumference?
188.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) 11 cm. [Circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference) uses d=22 cm — true?
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 22$ cm, so $C = \pi \times 22$ — **true**.

189.hint: Does the word arc mean the curved part of the circumference?
189.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? The word arc means curved part of [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference).
2. An **arc** is the **curved part** of the [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference) — **always**.
3. Use this check: curved edge between A and B.

190.hint: Concentric circles radii 3 and 5 — subtract for ring width.
190.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Two concentric circles radii 3 and 5. Width of ring between them?

3. Use this check: radii 4 and 7 → width 3 cm.

191.hint: Angle in a semicircle on the circumference — what size angle?
191.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

192.hint: 240° sector — what is the minor sector angle?
192.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

3. Use this check: pick the smaller wedge under 180°.

193.hint: Tangents from external point P — always equal in length?
193.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Always, sometimes, or never? Equal tangents from external point P.
2. Tangents from **external P** are **equal** — **always**.
3. Use this check: both 8 cm from P.

194.hint: Five equal pizza slices — divide 360° by 5 for central angle.
194.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Design a pizza problem with 5 equal slices — central angle?
2. $360 \div 5 = 72°$ per slice.
3. Use this check: four slices → 90° each.

195.hint: Does a radius meet the circle at exactly one point?
195.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: True or false? [Radius](mtlearn:word/radius) meets circle at one point only.
2. A **[radius](mtlearn:word/radius)** meets the circle at **one point** on the edge — **true**.
3. Use this check: spoke touches rim once.

196.hint: Explain why diameter equals two radii placed end to end.
196.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Explain why [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) = 2 × [radius](mtlearn:word/radius).
2. [Diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) goes **centre to edge twice** (both directions) → **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter) $= 2 \times$ [radius](mtlearn:word/radius)**.
3. Use this check: two 5 cm radii make 10 cm [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter).

197.hint: Centres 13 cm apart, radii 5 and 8 — do they touch externally?

**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Two circles centres 13 cm apart radii 5 and 8. Do they touch externally?
2. $5 + 8 = 13$ cm — centres exactly **sum of radii** → **touch externally** — **yes**.

198.hint: Tangent–radius triangle — where is the right angle?

**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Create a tangent–[radius](mtlearn:word/radius) right triangle story and label the right angle.
2. Right angle at **point of contact** where **[radius](mtlearn:word/radius) ⟂ tangent**.

199.hint: Regular hexagon inscribed — six equal arcs at the centre?
199.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Stretch: regular hexagon inscribed — each central angle at centre for six arcs?
2. Six equal arcs → central angle $360 \div 6 = 60°$ each.
3. Use this check: six equal pizza slices from centre.

200.hint: Link all circle words in one paragraph — start from the centre.
200.help:
**Topic guide:** [Learn this topic](mtlearn:geometry/G9)

1. Read what the question asks: Stretch: define all circle words (centre, [radius](mtlearn:word/radius), [diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter), chord, tangent, arc, sector, segment, [circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference)) in one linked paragraph.
2. From **centre**, **[radius](mtlearn:word/radius)** to edge; **[diameter](mtlearn:word/diameter)** is twice [radius](mtlearn:word/radius); **chord** joins two edge points; **tangent** touches once; **arc** is curved edge; **sector** is wedge; **segment** is chord+arc region; **[circumference](mtlearn:word/circumference)** is full edge.
3. Use this check: label each on one diagram.
